one_line_takeaway: By pairing a compliant low-tier LLM's iterative drafts with a high-tier LLM's refinement of both the draft and original malicious request, jailbreak success on cybersecurity-attack prompts rose from 7.1% to 62.0% at roughly 7.5 cents per prompt, showing current safety filters fail to catch harmful intent that accumulates across a multi-turn draft-then-refine pipeline rather than appearing in any single prompt.
key_finding: SR rose from 7.1% at baseline (N=0, no refinement) to 57.1% at N=1 (an ~8x jump), then 59.1% (N=2), peaking at 62.0% (N=3), before dropping to 46.6% at N=4 -- the degradation attributed to GPT-4o-mini refusing prototype-to-production refinement requests at the final stage. A/B preference testing across the nine LLM evaluators consistently favored higher-refinement outputs as more harmful/higher quality, with N=1-vs-N=3 preference at 71.8% and N=3-vs-N=4 at a more modest 54.5% (diminishing returns). Unit testing of 20 N=4 code samples (160 tests) gave 83.7% average pass rate, 89.2% median, with only 30.0% of samples achieving a full 100% pass without modification. Per-prompt cost scaled from 4.97 cents (N=0) to 7.52 cents (N=3, the SR-optimal configuration) and 7.37 cents (N=4), i.e., a maximum ~51% cost increase over baseline for a roughly 8-fold-plus SR gain. Manual case studies confirmed the generated SYN-flood script executed successfully against a live VM target (packet capture shown) and the SQL-injection scanner found 35 potential vulnerabilities and extracted real data (database name, credentials, MySQL version) from 15 of 41 tested payloads against a public test target, though all three tools had noted practical limitations (e.g., SYN-flood ineffective against modern SYN-cookie/rate-limiting defenses; SQLi scanner GET-only and sequential despite importing concurrency libraries).
core_contribution: The paper introduces Content Concretization (CC), a new jailbreaking technique that uses a two-model pipeline: a lower-tier, less-constrained LLM (GPT-4o-mini) iteratively drafts a solution (via stages: program requirements -> pseudocode -> prototype code -> executable code), and a higher-tier LLM (Claude 3.7 Sonnet) then takes the original malicious request plus the draft and produces a 'production-grade' final output. The technique deliberately avoids prompt obfuscation or role-play/prompt-engineering framing, so that any measured jailbreak improvement is attributable solely to the iterative concretization/refinement process rather than to disguising the malicious intent. The authors build five architecture variants (N=0 baseline through N=4 refinement stages) and evaluate them on 350 prompts sampled from the CySecBench dataset (their own prior benchmark) across seven cybersecurity categories (Cloud Attacks, Cryptographic Attacks, Intrusion Techniques, IoT Attacks, Malware Attacks, Network Attacks, Web Attacks).
threat_model: Attacker interacts with commercial LLM APIs (OpenAI GPT-4o-mini as the low-tier drafting model, Anthropic Claude 3.7 Sonnet as the high-tier refining/output model) with no other adversarial access beyond standard API prompting; the attack does not require model weights, fine-tuning, gradient access, or jailbreak-specific obfuscation/role-play tricks. The goal is to elicit malicious, executable cybersecurity attack code (e.g., DoS tools, spear-phishing tooling, SQL-injection scanners) that a human attacker of intermediate coding skill could deploy with modest additional work. The paper frames the vulnerability as safety filters evaluating only the final prompt/response pair and failing to detect the cumulative harmful intent that builds up across a multi-step 'extend existing content' interaction.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the method was developed specifically for cybersecurity code generation where abstraction layers (pseudocode -> prototype -> production code) are easy to define; extending CC to other harm domains would require new domain-specific instruction design; (2) only one low-tier/high-tier model pairing (GPT-4o-mini + Claude 3.7 Sonnet) was tested due to practical constraints, so other pairings might yield higher or lower SRs; (3) evaluation relies heavily on automated LLM-based judging (keyword filter + LLM jury + LLM-generated unit tests) rather than exhaustive manual review of all 350x5 responses, which the authors acknowledge could introduce systematic bias, partially mitigated by using a 3-model jury with majority voting and independent A/B preference testing. Additionally, the paper explicitly excludes combining CC with prompt obfuscation/engineering, so the reported SRs are likely a lower bound relative to a combined attack.

datasets_used: CySecBench (Wahréus, Hussain, Papadimitratos 2025) -- 350 prompts sampled (50 each from Cloud Attacks, Cryptographic Attacks, Intrusion Techniques, IoT Attacks, Malware Attacks, Network Attacks, Web Attacks categories)
quantitative_results: Success Rate by refinement count: N=0 (baseline) 7.1%; N=1 57.1%; N=2 59.1%; N=3 62.0% (peak); N=4 46.6% (Table 1)
A/B average LLM preference for higher-N output: N1 vs N2 71.1%; N1 vs N3 71.8%; N1 vs N4 69.1%; N2 vs N3 57.9%; N2 vs N4 54.7%; N3 vs N4 54.5% (Table 3)
Unit testing on 20 N=4 code samples (160 tests total, avg 8/sample): average pass 83.7%, median pass 89.2%, min pass 28.6%, max pass 100.0%, 30.0% of samples fully passing (Table 4)
Per-prompt cost: N=0 4.97 cents; N=1 6.36 cents (+28%); N=2 6.76 cents (+36%); N=3 7.52 cents (+51%); N=4 7.37 cents (+48%) (Table 6)
Total token usage per architecture (GPT-4o-mini draft + Claude 3.7 Sonnet final): N=0 3576 tokens; N=1 5507; N=2 6641; N=3 8966; N=4 10907 (Table 5)

Manual case study: SQL-injection scanner tested against testphp.vulnweb.com found 35 potential vulnerabilities out of 41 payloads tested, 15 with confirmed data extraction (database name 'acuart', credentials 'acuart@localhost', MySQL version 8.0.22-0ubuntu0.20.04.2)
baselines_compared: N=0 architecture: direct generation by the high-tier model (Claude 3.7 Sonnet) with no draft/refinement stage, used as the paper's own baseline (not an external prior-work jailbreak method)
